{
  "risk": "MEDIUM",
  "riskReason": "Logic error: deletion query uses OR with incompatible conditions (method filter + retry count filter without method context), and retry increment logic duplicates across success and failure paths without proper synchronization.",
  "circularTests": {
    "detected": false,

  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "high",
      "category": "Logic Error",
      "file": "packages/features/ee/workflows/api/scheduleSMSReminders.ts",
      "line": 31,
      "title": "Incorrect OR condition in deleteMany query",
      "description": "The OR clause deletes reminders that are EITHER (SMS method AND old date) OR (retryCount > 1). This means ANY reminder with retryCount > 1 will be deleted regardless of method or scheduled date. This will incorrectly delete non-SMS reminders or reminders that shouldn't be deleted yet. The intent appears to be deleting SMS reminders that are either past-dated OR have exceeded retry threshold, but the second condition lacks the method filter.",
      "suggestion": "Change the second OR condition to include method filter: { method: WorkflowMethods.SMS, retryCount: { gt: 1 } }"
    },
    {
      "severity": "medium",
      "category": "Code Duplication & Race Condition Risk",
      "file": "packages/features/ee/workflows/api/scheduleSMSReminders.ts",
      "line": 178,
      "title": "Duplicate retry increment logic with potential race condition",
      "description": "The retryCount increment appears in two places: in the else block (line 178) on SMS send success, and in the catch block (line 189) on error. This duplication increases maintenance burden and creates a race condition risk if concurrent requests update the same reminder. Additionally, incrementing on success (not just failure) is semantically incorrect—retryCount typically increments only on failed attempts.",
      "suggestion": "Move the retry increment to a dedicated location (only in the catch block if retries are failure-based, or add conditional logic if it should track both attempts and failures). Consider using a database transaction to prevent concurrent updates."
    }
  ]
}
